name: Continue
description: Open-source, highly configurable IDE extension agent

developer.comment: Founded June 2023 by Ty Dunn and Nate Sesti (Y Combinator S23).

first-release.comment: Open-source VS Code extension first released July 2023; v1.0 launched later. Founded June 2023. Month is approximate from public history (checked 2026-06-02).

status.comment: Actively developed; 33.5k GitHub stars, 472 contributors as of 2026-06-02.

license.comment: Repository licensed Apache 2.0 (© 2023-2024 Continue Dev, Inc.). The hosted Continue Hub/platform adds paid tiers but the IDE extension is OSS.

specialization.comment: Broad coding assistant (chat, autocomplete, edit, agent); not positioned for a single niche.

interface.comment: Primarily VS Code and JetBrains extensions; also ships an open-source CLI tool (cn).

host-ides.comment: Extensions for VS Code and JetBrains IDEs, plus a CLI that runs in the terminal.

self-contained-vs-plugin.comment: Editor extension (VS Code/JetBrains) and a CLI tool; not a standalone editor.

default-model-provider.comment: Model-agnostic by design; the user configures a provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Ollama, Mistral, xAI, Bedrock, etc.). No single vendor-locked default.

model-choice.comment: Switch among many models/providers via config.yaml; distinct models per role (chat, edit, autocomplete, embed).

bring-your-own-key.comment: Users supply their own API keys for any supported provider.

local-model-support.comment: Works with Ollama, LM Studio, Jan, vLLM and other local/OpenAI-compatible endpoints; local embeddings supported too.

multi-file-editing.comment: Agent mode applies coordinated edits across multiple files.

autonomous-task-execution.comment: Agent mode chains tool calls (edit, run, read) toward a goal; tools sent with each request and the model decides which to call.

terminal-command-execution.comment: Agent mode can run terminal/shell commands as tools, gated by tool policies.

mcp-support.comment: MCP servers can be added to give Agent mode additional tools; servers discoverable via the Continue Hub.

subagents-parallel-agents.comment: No documented spawning of sub-agents or parallel workers; Agent mode runs a single tool-calling loop. Marked null (no clear vendor confirmation either way) as of 2026-06-02.

codebase-indexing.comment: Builds an embeddings-based semantic index (default all-MiniLM-L6-v2, stored locally in ~/.continue/index) combined with keyword search; exposed via @codebase.

planning-mode.comment: Has a Plan mode that includes only read-only tools, distinct from Agent (all tools) and Chat (no tools).

multimodal-input.comment: Accepts image input when the configured model is vision-capable (e.g. Claude 3.5+, GPT-4o, Gemini, some Ollama vision models).

browser-computer-use.comment: No documented built-in browser/GUI-driving tool to act and verify changes; only achievable via external MCP servers. Marked null (not a shipped core capability) as of 2026-06-02.

autonomous-subagent-spawning.comment: Agent mode runs a single tool-calling loop (tools sent with each request; the model picks one to call) with no documented mechanism to spawn or delegate to subagents on its own initiative. Per RESEARCH.md guideline, null/false follows from subagents-parallel-agents being null (no subagent capability confirmed either way). Checked 2026-06-14.

operating-mode.comment: Tab autocomplete, conversational chat/edit, and a supervised Agent mode that asks permission for tools by default.

inline-completion.comment: Genuine as-you-type tab autocomplete via a dedicated autocomplete model role.

background-async-execution.comment: IDE extension agent runs in-session; no documented detached/cloud background task runner. Marked null as of 2026-06-02.

persistent-memory.comment: Rules provide always-on project context, but no documented cross-session learned memory mechanism. Marked null (no clear confirmation of persistent learned memory) as of 2026-06-02.

checkpoints-rollback.comment: No documented built-in checkpoint/snapshot-restore for an agent run as a unit; relies on the user's own git. Marked null as of 2026-06-02.

git-integration.comment: Diff context and a @git-style context provider; edits surface as reviewable diffs. The newer PR-checks product also integrates with GitHub status checks.

pr-creation.comment: Continue's CI checks integrate with GitHub pull requests, suggesting diffs you accept/reject from the PR; not a core IDE-extension feature per se.

custom-rules-instructions.comment: Supports rules as YAML config, plain text, or Markdown (.md) files applied to Agent, Chat, and Edit.

hooks-extensibility.comment: Extensible via MCP servers, custom context providers, prompts/slash commands, and the config.yaml/Hub ecosystem.

permission-model.comment: Agent mode asks permission before using a tool by default; tool policies let the user exclude or auto-allow specific tools.

self-host-on-prem.comment: Fully local/offline operation is supported (local models + local embeddings); open-source extension runs entirely on the user's machine.

privacy-mode.comment: Open-source extension does not train on code; it only sends anonymized, PII-stripped usage telemetry to PostHog, which is opt-out. Running local models keeps code fully on-device.

enterprise-controls.comment: Company (Enterprise) plan offers custom SSO via SAML or OIDC and BYOK; audit logs / RBAC / VPC not explicitly listed on the pricing page (checked 2026-06-02).

pricing-model.comment: Core OSS extension is free (you pay your own model/API costs, i.e. BYOK). Hub platform: Starter pay-as-you-go ($3/M tokens), Team ($20/seat/mo), Company custom (checked 2026-06-02).

free-tier.comment: Open-source extension is free to use with your own/local models; covers core coding use.

entry-price-usd-mo.comment: Core extension is free for full coding use (BYO model). Paid Hub tiers start at Team $20/seat/mo; Starter is pay-as-you-go ($3/M tokens) with no fixed plan.

maturity.comment: Mature, widely used OSS extension (33.5k stars) past v1.0, but config/feature surface still evolves rapidly; not a perfect 5.

community-size.comment: Per RESEARCH.md thresholds, Continue sits in the medium band (alongside Cline, Amazon Q); 33.5k GitHub stars.

extension-plugin-ecosystem.comment: Continue Hub marketplace for shared models, rules, prompts, MCP servers, and agents.

project-memory-files.comment: Auto-loads rule files from a vendor-specific .continue/rules folder (workspace) and ~/.continue/rules (global), as plain text/Markdown/YAML. Does not auto-load AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, or copilot-instructions by default; root-AGENTS.md support is only a proposed/open issue (#6716) as of 2026-06-02. Tagged custom for the .continue/rules convention.

memory-scopes.comment: Global user rules in ~/.continue/rules (user scope) and per-workspace rules in .continue/rules (project scope). No documented enterprise/org policy scope or per-subfolder rule placement (path scoping is done via globs, not folder location); checked 2026-06-02.

scoped-rules-files.comment: Rules support glob patterns (e.g. **/*.{ts,tsx}) so they activate on matching file context (path-based, also covers file-type via extension globs); alwaysApply:false lets the agent pull a rule in on demand based on its description (agent-requested); alwaysApply:true / no frontmatter applies to every interaction (always-on).

auto-memory.comment: Rules provide always-on project context but are authored manually; no documented mechanism by which Continue writes cross-session memory on its own. Marked unsupported as of 2026-06-02.

skill-support.comment: Reusable prompts/slash commands defined as Markdown-with-frontmatter (invokable:true) plus shareable agents via the Hub; Continue also scans .continue/skills and .claude/skills directories for Claude Code-style skills (checked 2026-06-02).

skill-invocation.comment: Slash-command prompts are user-triggered via /name in Chat/Plan/Agent. Skills loaded from .claude/skills can be model-invoked (the open request to add a disable-model-invocation field implies model invocation is active), though the prompt vs skill UX is currently fragmented per discussion #12127.

always-on-surface.comment: Claude Code-style skills carry name + description with the body pulled in on invocation (progressive disclosure); slash-command prompts surface by name in the / menu. Community notes Continue's progressive-disclosure handling is still incomplete vs Claude Code (discussion #12127). Best-fit: description-only.

skill-distribution.comment: Continue Hub provides a curated, browsable, one-click registry of prompts/rules/agents (marketplace); skills/prompts can also be dropped into local directories (.continue/skills, .claude/skills, .continue/prompts) by hand (manual-files).

sandboxing-technology.comment: No documented OS sandbox, container, or VM isolation for tool/command execution; the Bash and write tools run with the user's full privileges and Continue relies on the permission-prompt handshake alone. Docs warn 'Be careful setting tools to automatic if their behavior is not read-only.' Checked 2026-06-12.

permission-granularity.comment: Tool policies match per tool (e.g. Write), by command prefix/glob (e.g. Bash(npm install*)), and by path glob (e.g. Write(**/*.ts)). Read-only tools default to allow while write tools (Edit/MultiEdit/Write) and Bash default to ask, giving a read/write distinction. Checked 2026-06-12.

rule-precedence.comment: Permissions resolve through an explicit source priority order (mode policies --auto/--readonly > CLI flags > ~/.continue/permissions.yaml > built-in defaults); the highest-priority source wins. Rules are pattern-policy list entries (allow/ask/exclude). Intra-file conflict resolution (specificity vs first-match) is not documented, so single-picked the source-ordered model as the most expressive documented rung. Checked 2026-06-12.

permission-configurability.comment: Users author allow/ask/exclude tool policies (with tool + command/path glob matching) in ~/.continue/permissions.yaml, via CLI flags, or in the IDE tool settings. No documented programmable permission callback/policy script that inspects each concrete action, so declarative-rules is the highest level reached. Checked 2026-06-12.

sensitive-file-protection.comment: No shipped default deny patterns for secrets (.env, key files); the user can write exclude rules (e.g. exclude Read of .env) but none ship enabled. Checked 2026-06-12.

persistent-allowlist.comment: Approving a tool with 'Continue + don't ask again' writes a policy to ~/.continue/permissions.yaml, which persists across sessions and restarts. Checked 2026-06-12.

unsupervised-mode.comment: The CLI (cn) --auto flag allows all tools without prompting ('Allows everything — *: allow'). IDE Agent mode can also set tools to Automatic to skip approval. Checked 2026-06-12.

network-access-control.comment: No documented outbound-network gating during a run; Fetch and Bash run with the user's full network access (no domain allowlist, proxy, or block). The user could exclude network tools, but egress is unrestricted by default. Checked 2026-06-12.

default-permission-posture.comment: Out of the box, read-only tools auto-allow but write tools (Edit/MultiEdit/Write) AND Bash default to 'ask' — so every file edit and every command is prompted (Ask First is the IDE default). Best-fit prompt-each-action rather than auto-edit-ask-commands, since edits are not auto-applied. Checked 2026-06-12.

auto-accept-mode.comment: IDE Agent mode exposes tool policies (Ask First / Automatic / Excluded) adjustable in-session via the tools icon, with groups toggled on/off — so edit/write tools can be set to Automatic (auto-apply edits) while Bash stays on Ask First, giving the intermediate auto-accept-edits middle rung distinct from full bypass. In the CLI (cn), Shift+Tab cycles Normal/Plan/Auto but its only non-prompting rung is full --auto bypass (counted under Unsupervised Mode). True on the strength of the IDE's granular in-session auto-approval, though it is per-tool configuration rather than a single named one-keystroke 'auto-accept' toggle. Checked 2026-07-18.
